title: Why Is My Pothos Turning Yellow? 7 Common Causes and Fixes
excerpt: Yellow leaves on your pothos? Learn the 7 most common reasons and how to fix each one to get your plant back to full health.
content:
Why Are My Pothos Leaves Turning Yellow?

Pothos (Epipremnum aureum) is one of the hardiest houseplants around, which is why yellow leaves can feel so frustrating. The good news? Yellow leaves almost always point to a fixable problem. Here are the seven most common causes.

1. Overwatering

This is the #1 reason for yellow pothos leaves. When roots sit in soggy soil, they can't absorb oxygen, leading to root rot and yellowing.

Fix: Let the top 1-2 inches of soil dry out between waterings. Make sure your pot has drainage holes. If the soil smells musty, check the roots — trim any brown, mushy ones and repot in fresh soil.

2. Underwatering

While pothos tolerates some neglect, prolonged drought causes older leaves to turn yellow and crispy.

Fix: Water thoroughly until it drains from the bottom. Establish a consistent watering schedule — most pothos need water every 7-10 days.

3. Too Much Direct Sunlight

Pothos prefer bright, indirect light. Direct sun can scorch leaves, causing yellow or brown patches.

Fix: Move your plant a few feet from the window or use a sheer curtain to filter light. North or east-facing windows work best.

4. Nutrient Deficiency

If your pothos has been in the same soil for over a year without feeding, it may lack essential nutrients like nitrogen.

Fix: Feed with a balanced liquid balanced liquid fertilizer (10-10-10 fertilizer) once a month during spring and summer. Reduce to every 6-8 weeks in fall and winter.

5. Temperature Stress

Pothos thrive between 65-85°F (18-29°C). Cold drafts, AC vents, or sudden temperature swings can trigger yellowing.

Fix: Keep your plant away from drafty windows, exterior doors, and HVAC vents. Maintain a stable room temperature.

6. Root Bound

If roots are circling the bottom of the pot and poking out of drainage holes, your pothos may be root-bound, limiting nutrient uptake.

Fix: Repot into a container 1-2 inches larger in diameter with fresh quality potting mix.

7. Natural Aging

Sometimes the oldest leaves at the base of the plant simply yellow and drop as part of the plant's natural growth cycle. If it's just 1-2 leaves and new growth looks healthy, don't worry.

Fix: No action needed. Simply remove the yellow leaves to keep your plant looking tidy.

When to Worry

If multiple leaves are yellowing rapidly, check for root rot immediately. Remove the plant from its pot, trim damaged roots, and repot in fresh, well-draining soil. With the right care, your pothos will bounce back quickly.
